attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a widespread rash that developed over the past 24 hours. She started taking amoxicillin two days ago for a dental infection. She has chronic back pain and takes oxycodone regularly.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There are numerous small pustules on an erythematous base across her trunk and limbs, with a larger concentration in her skin folds. The rash is painful and pruritic. There is no mucosal involvement.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations would be most helpful in confirming the diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis (AGEP or toxic pustoloderma), a severe cutaneous adverse reaction (SCAR) typically occurring within 48 hours of drug exposure. The presentation of fever, widespread non-follicular pustules on an erythematous base, and recent antibiotic use is characteristic, with more than 90% of AGEP being triggered by medications. The lack of mucosal involvement also points to AGEP instead of Steven Johnson syndrome/ toxic epidermal necrolysis.

Punch biopsy of affected skin is the most helpful investigation to confirm the diagnosis of AGEP. Histopathology typically shows spongiform subcorneal pustules with a neutrophilic infiltrate in the dermis. These findings, combined with the clinical presentation, are diagnostic.

Antinuclear antibody testing would not be helpful in this acute presentation. While autoimmune conditions can present with skin manifestations, the rapid onset and characteristic pustular eruption make AGEP more likely.

Skin swab for bacterial culture would show sterile pustules in AGEP. While this might help exclude impetigo or other bacterial infections, it is not the most definitive test for diagnosis, and would not aid in treatment which involves abrupt cessation of the causative medication (antibiotic in this case).

Serum tryptase level would be more appropriate if anaphylaxis was suspected. While AGEP is a drug reaction, it is not an IgE-mediated process, and tryptase levels would not be elevated.

Patch testing can be performed several weeks after recovery to identify the causative drug but is not useful in the acute setting for diagnosis. Additionally, its sensitivity for AGEP is variable, and there is clear history of amoxycillin use being the trigger in this case.

Other supportive investigations would include full blood count (which typically shows neutrophilia), inflammatory markers (raised), liver function tests, and renal function (may be mildly abnormal). The causative medication should be ceased immediately and resolution of symptoms with supportive management is generally expected over the following ten days.
learningPoints: Acute generalised exanthematous pustulosis typically presents with widespread non-follicular sterile pustules on an erythematous base within 48 hours of drug exposure.
Skin biopsy showing spongiform subcorneal pustules with neutrophilic infiltrate is diagnostic for AGEP.
references: Purvis D, Oakley A.  Acute generalised exanthematous pustolosis. DermNet NZ. 2015.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/acute-generalised-exanthematous-pustulosis

Moreno-Arrones OM, Carrillo-Gijon R, Sendagorta E, Rios-Buceta L. Acute generalized exanthematous pustulosis simulating Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis associated with the use of vismodegib. Journal of American Academy of Dermatology Case Rep. 2018 Jan 16;4(2):123-125.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5789517/#:~:text=SJS%2FTEN%20overlap%20is%20defined,withdrawal%20of%20the%20culprit%20drug.
